mainTopic: (continues) אחריות טעות סופר · שדה שאני לוקח · המוצא שטר הקנאה בשוק
overview: (1) A discussion on the liability for a scribe's error in documents, leading to a debate on the validity of selling future acquisitions and the concept of 'trust' (הימנותא) in transactions. (2) Rav's ruling that one can sell land they intend to buy in the future, based on the principle that a person can transfer something not yet in existence. (3) The laws regarding finding a deed of acquisition or a debt note in the market, debating whether they must be returned to the owner and the concerns regarding payment (פרעון).

sugyaBoundaries.gist: A discussion on the liability for a scribe's error in documents, leading to a debate on the validity of selling future acquisitions and the concept of 'trust' (הימנותא) in transactions.
Rav's ruling that one can sell land they intend to buy in the future, based on the principle that a person can transfer something not yet in existence.
The laws regarding finding a deed of acquisition or a debt note in the market, debating whether they must be returned to the owner and the concerns regarding payment (פרעון).

steps.title: רַב's ruling on selling a field one intends to buy
steps.stepSummary: Rav Huna cites Rav's ruling that a person can sell a field they intend to buy in the future, with the transfer taking effect retroactively from the moment of the agreement.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a freestanding ruling (מימרא) attributed to רַב via רַב הוּנָא, establishing the baseline halakhic position on selling future property. This is a setup for the subsequent discussion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The ruling introduces the concept of selling a field not yet owned. The key mechanism is retroactive acquisition, meaning the sale is valid once the seller actually acquires the field. This sets the stage for the subsequent discussion on the scope of this ruling.

keyTerms.meaning: A field that I am about to buy
It is acquired for you from now (retroactively)
He has acquired

phrases.english: Rav Huna says that Rav says:
With regard to one who says to another: With regard to the field that I am about to buy,
when I buy it, it will be retroactively transferred to your ownership from now, the stipulation takes effect, and once he buys it, the second party has acquired the field.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַב הוּנָא אָמַר רַב: הָאוֹמֵר לַחֲבֵרוֹ שָׂדֶה שֶׁאֲנִי לוֹקֵחַ, לִכְשֶׁאקָּחֶנָּה, קְנוּיָה לָךְ מֵעַכְשָׁיו – קָנָה.

rashi.hebrew: קנויה לך מעכשיו - שכשאקחנה לא אוכל לחזור בו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase 'קנויה לך מעכשיו' means that once the seller acquires the field, he cannot retract from the sale.

rashi.hebrew: קנה - אם יקחנה ואין יכול לחזור כשלקחה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'קנה' means the buyer acquires the field if the seller actually purchases it, and the seller cannot retract at that point.

tosafot.hebrew: שדה שאני לוקח - ל"ג זו מדקאמר והאלהים אפילו בשדה זו הלא בפירוש אמר זו:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes a textual variant suggesting the Gemara should read 'זו' (this field) rather than 'שדה שאני לוקח', based on the later mention of 'והאלהים'.

tosafot.hebrew: קנויה לך מעכשיו - תימה מה צריך מעכשיו כיון דאדם מקנה דבר שלא בא לעולם דאי לעניין חזרה הא כל זמן שלא בא לעולם יכול לחזור אע"ג דאמר מעכשיו וי"ל דנ"מ אם נקרע השטר או שאבד קודם שלקחה זה דקני כיון דאמר מעכשיו:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions the necessity of 'מעכשיו' since one can acquire דבר שלא בא לעולם anyway, and answers that it matters if the document is torn or lost before acquisition, as 'מעכשיו' ensures the buyer still acquires the field.

steps.title: רַבָּא's limitation to an unspecified field
steps.stepSummary: Rava limits Rav's ruling to an unspecified field, arguing that selling a specific field one does not own is invalid because the seller cannot guarantee the current owner will sell it to him.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא introduces a distinction to limit רַב's ruling, differentiating between an unspecified field and a specific one. This is a setup line narrowing the scope of the previous discussion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא argues that רַב's statement only applies to a general field because a person has the general ability to buy land. For a specific field, the transaction fails because the seller lacks control over the current owner's willingness to sell. This introduces the concept that selling something entirely outside one's control is like selling a non-existent entity.

keyTerms.meaning: An unspecified field
This specific field

keyTerms.term: מִי יֵימַר דְּמִזְבֵּן לַהּ נִיחָא לֵיהּ
keyTerms.meaning: Who can say that he will sell it to him?

steps.whatToRemember: רַבָּא distinguishes between 'שָׂדֶה סְתָם' (valid) and 'שָׂדֶה זוֹ' (invalid) based on the seller's control.
steps.confusionAlert: רַבָּא is not rejecting רַב outright but limiting his ruling to a specific context.
steps.whyThisMatters: This distinction raises the question of whether רַב's ruling relies on the seller's general ability to acquire property or on a broader principle of transferring future assets.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא: מִסְתַּבְּרָא מִלְּתֵאּ דְרַב בְּשָׂדֶה סְתָם.
phrases.english: Rava said: The statement of Rav is reasonable only with regard to an unspecified field, as one is capable of buying a field.
But with regard to a case where one says to another that he is selling him this specific field that is not yet in his possession, the transaction does not take effect,
as who is to say that the current owner will sell it to him?

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רָבָא: מִסְתַּבְּרָא מִלְּתֵאּ דְרַב בְּשָׂדֶה סְתָם. אֲבָל בְּשָׂדֶה זוֹ – לָא. מִי יֵימַר דִּמְזַבֵּין לַהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: בשדה סתם - שדה שאני לוקח ולא אמר זו דסמכא דעתיה דמקבל מתנה לסמוך עליו שיקח שדה ויתננה לו שהרבה שדות מצויות ליקח אבל אמר לו שדה זו לא סמכא דעתיה דמקבל מתנה ולא האמינו דנימא ניחא ליה דליקו בהימנותיה ולא מסתבר כוותיה דרב בהא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in a case of שדה סתם, the recipient can rely on the giver's ability to purchase land since many fields are available, but in a case of שדה זו, the recipient cannot rely on the giver because the giver lacks control over the specific owner's willingness to sell, making Rav's ruling inapplicable.

steps.title: Rejection of רַבָּא's limitation via רַבִּי מֵאִיר's principle
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara rejects Rava's limitation, asserting that Rav's ruling applies even to a specific field, based on Rabbi Meir's principle that one can transfer ownership of something not yet in existence.
steps.whatsHappening: The גְּמָרָא forcefully rejects רַבָּא's distinction, using an oath to emphasize that רַב's ruling is broader than רַבָּא assumed. This is a direct challenge to the previous step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גְּמָרָא invokes רַבִּי מֵאִיר's principle to resolve the difficulty. Since רַב follows רַבִּי מֵאִיר, the sale of a specific field is valid because the principle of transferring future assets applies regardless of the seller's control over the current owner. This shifts the focus from the seller's ability to buy to the legal capacity to transfer future property.

keyTerms.meaning: By God (emphatic rejection)
In accordance with whom did רַב rule?

keyTerms.term: אָדָם מַקְנֶה דָּבָר שֶׁלֹּא בָּא לָעוֹלָם
keyTerms.meaning: A person can transfer ownership of something not yet in existence

steps.whatToRemember: רַב follows רַבִּי מֵאִיר, who allows transferring 'דָּבָר שֶׁלֹּא בָּא לָעוֹלָם'.
steps.confusionAlert: The oath ('וְהָאֱלֹהִים') signals a strong rejection of רַבָּא's reading, not just a mild correction.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes that רַב's ruling is based on a broader legal principle rather than a practical assessment of the seller's control.

phrases.english: By God! Rav said

phrases.aramaic: אֲפִילּוּ בְּ״שָׂדֶה זוֹ״ מִכְּדֵי רַב כְּמַאן
phrases.english: even in a case where the seller said: This field
After all, in accordance with whose opinion did Rav state his halakha?
It was in accordance with the opinion of Rabbi Meir, who says that

phrases.aramaic: אָדָם מַקְנֶה דָּבָר שֶׁלֹּא בָּא לָעוֹלָם.
phrases.english: a person can transfer ownership of an entity that has not yet come into the world.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָאֱלֹהִים! אָמַר רַב: אֲפִילּוּ בְּ״שָׂדֶה זוֹ״ מִכְּדֵי רַב כְּמַאן אַמְרַהּ לִשְׁמַעְתֵּיהּ – כְּרַבִּי מֵאִיר, דְּאָמַר: אָדָם מַקְנֶה דָּבָר שֶׁלֹּא בָּא לָעוֹלָם.

rashi.hebrew: והאלהים - בשבועה רב אמרה אפילו בשדה זו דמכדי רב כמאן אמר לשמעתיה כר"מ כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's oath (והאלהים) emphasizes that רב's ruling applies even to a specific field, because רב follows רבי מאיר's principle that one can acquire something not yet in existence.

steps.title: בְּרֵיתָא on betrothal after future events
steps.stepSummary: A baraita is cited to support Rabbi Meir's principle, showing that betrothal can be valid even when contingent on future events like conversion, freedom, or death.
steps.whatsHappening: The גְּמָרָא brings a בְּרֵיתָא as proof for רַבִּי מֵאִיר's position, listing various cases where betrothal is contingent on future events. This is a textual proof step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא presents a dispute between the חֲכָמִים and רַבִּי מֵאִיר regarding betrothal contingent on future events. The חֲכָמִים invalidate such betrothals because the woman is not currently available for betrothal. רַבִּי מֵאִיר, however, validates them, demonstrating his principle that one can transfer ownership of something not yet available.

keyTerms.meaning: Be betrothed to me after I convert
She is not betrothed
רַבִּי מֵאִיר says: She is betrothed

steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרֵיתָא lists cases like 'after I convert' or 'after your husband dies' to illustrate the dispute.
steps.confusionAlert: The בְּרֵיתָא covers multiple scenarios, but the core issue is the same: can a transaction be valid if the subject is not currently available?
steps.whyThisMatters: This בְּרֵיתָא provides the textual basis for רַבִּי מֵאִיר's principle, which is crucial for understanding רַב's ruling.

phrases.aramaic: דְּתַנְיָא: הָאוֹמֵר לְאִשָּׁה ״הִתְקַדְּשִׁי לִי לְאַחַר שֶׁאֶתְגַּיֵּיר״,
phrases.english: As it is taught in a baraita: With regard to one who says to a woman: Be betrothed to me after I convert,
or: After you convert, or if he is a slave and says: After I am freed,
or if she is a maidservant and he says: After you are freed, or if he says to a married woman: After your husband dies,
or if he says to a widow waiting for her yavam to perform the ritual through which he frees her from her levirate bonds [ḥalitza]: After your yavam performs ḥalitza with you,
or if he says to his wife’s sister: After your sister dies (see Leviticus 18:18), in all these cases she is not betrothed.
Rabbi Meir says: She is betrothed.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּתַנְיָא: הָאוֹמֵר לְאִשָּׁה ״הִתְקַדְּשִׁי לִי לְאַחַר שֶׁאֶתְגַּיֵּיר״, ״לְאַחַר שֶׁתִּתְגַּיְּירִי״, ״לְאַחַר שֶׁאֶשְׁתַּחְרֵר״, ״לְאַחַר שֶׁתִּשְׁתַּחְרְרִי״, ״לְאַחַר שֶׁיָּמוּת בַּעְלִיךְ״, ״לְאַחַר שֶׁיַּחְלוֹץ לָךְ יְבָמִיךְ״, ״לְאַחַר שֶׁתָּמוּת אֲחוֹתִיךְ״ – אֵינָהּ מְקוּדֶּשֶׁת. רַבִּי מֵאִיר אוֹמֵר: מְקוּדֶּשֶׁת.

steps.title: Conclusion: Specific field is like a specific woman
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara concludes that selling a specific field is analogous to betrothing a specific woman, and since Rabbi Meir validates the latter, Rav validates the former.
steps.whatsHappening: The גְּמָרָא draws a final conclusion by comparing the case of selling a specific field to the case of betrothing a specific woman. This is a concluding step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גְּמָרָא notes that a specific woman is analogous to a specific field. Since רַבִּי מֵאִיר holds that betrothal is valid even when contingent on future events, רַב, who follows רַבִּי מֵאִיר, must also hold that selling a specific field is valid. This resolves the initial question and confirms that רַב's ruling applies broadly, regardless of the seller's control over the property.

keyTerms.meaning: And isn't a woman comparable to 'this field'?
And yet רַבִּי מֵאִיר says: She is betrothed

steps.whatToRemember: The analogy between 'אִשָּׁה' and 'שָׂדֶה זוֹ' confirms רַב's broad ruling.
steps.confusionAlert: The comparison highlights that the legal principle, not the practical circumstances, determines the validity of the transaction.
steps.whyThisMatters: This conclusion solidifies the application of רַבִּי מֵאִיר's principle to property transactions, establishing a key precedent in הֲלָכָה.

phrases.english: And isn’t the case of betrothing a specific woman comparable to the case of selling this specific field?
And yet, Rabbi Meir says that she is betrothed.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָא אִשָּׁה כְּ״שָׂדֶה זוֹ״ דָּמְיָא, וְאָמַר רַבִּי מֵאִיר: מְקוּדֶּשֶׁת.

rashi.hebrew: והא אשה כשדה זו דמיא - דיש באלו שאין בידו להביא עצמו לכלל קדושין כאשר אין ביד זה לכוף את בעל השדה למוכרה לו דעבד ושפחה אין בידם לשחרר עצמם ואין ביד אשה להמית את בעלה או אחותה ואמר רבי מאיר דלכשיבאו לכלל קדושין מקודשת היא למפרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the analogy between a specific woman and a specific field is based on the fact that in both cases, the party initiating the transaction lacks the power to force the completion of the act (a woman cannot force her husband to divorce her or kill him, just as a buyer cannot force the owner to sell), yet רבי מאיר rules that the betrothal is valid retroactively once the condition is fulfilled.

steps.title: שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling on returning a found deed of acquisition
steps.stepSummary: Shmuel rules that a found deed of acquisition (שטר הקנאה) must be returned to the creditor, dismissing concerns about prior repayment because the debtor would have torn it up.
steps.whatsHappening: The סוּגְיָא opens with שְׁמוּאֵל's foundational ruling regarding a found document. This is a setup line introducing a new topic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: שְׁמוּאֵל establishes that a שִׁטְרָא דְּהַקְנָאָה must be returned. He preemptively addresses two potential objections: 1) The debtor might not have actually borrowed the money, but שְׁמוּאֵל dismisses this because the debtor bound himself at the time of writing. 2) The debt might have been repaid, but שְׁמוּאֵל dismisses this because the debtor would have torn up the note.

keyTerms.meaning: A deed of acquisition that creates a lien on property from the date of writing.
Repayment of a debt.
He bound himself (to pay even if the loan wasn't disbursed).
He would have torn it up (upon repayment).

steps.whatToRemember: The core principle here is that we do not worry about repayment for a שִׁטְרָא דְּהַקְנָאָה because the debtor had the ability to tear it up.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling applies specifically to a שִׁטְרָא דְּהַקְנָאָה, which creates a lien on property from the date of writing.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets the baseline for the entire סוּגְיָא: the tension between returning a document and the risk that it has already been paid off.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל: הַמּוֹצֵא שְׁטַר הַקְנָאָה בַּשּׁוּק –
phrases.english: Shmuel says: With regard to one who finds a deed of transfer, i.e., a promissory note that establishes a lien on the debtor’s property from the date it is written, regardless of whether or not he borrows the money at that time, in the marketplace,
he must return it to its owner, i.e., the creditor,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי מִשּׁוּם דְּכָתַב לִלְוֹת וְלֹא לָוָה –
phrases.english: as, if one were to be concerned because of the possibility that the debtor wrote the note intending to borrow money, but did not borrow it in the end,
he is nevertheless liable, since he committed himself to pay at the time it was written.
And if one were to be concerned because of the possibility that repayment had already taken place,
this is not a justified concern, as in general we are not concerned that there was repayment,
as, if it were so that the debtor had repaid it,
he certainly would have torn up the note.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר שמואל: המוצא שטר הקנאה בשוק – יחזירו לבעלים.

rashi.hebrew: שטר הקנאה - שקנו ממנו שאפילו לא ילוה המעות שיעבד עצמו לשלמם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that שטר הקנאה refers to a document where the debtor bound himself to pay even if he did not actually receive the loan proceeds.

steps.title: רַב נַחְמָן's anecdotal proof for שְׁמוּאֵל
steps.stepSummary: Rav Nachman recalls hearing an announcement in his youth that found deeds of acquisition should be returned, supporting Shmuel's ruling.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב נַחְמָן provides a practical, anecdotal proof for שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling. This is a proof step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב נַחְמָן recalls that his father was a scribe for the judges of מַר שְׁמוּאֵל. When he was six or seven, he heard an announcement that found deeds of acquisition should be returned to their owners. This serves as a proof that the practical הֲלָכָה follows שְׁמוּאֵל.

keyTerms.meaning: The scribes of the judges of מַר שְׁמוּאֵל.
They made an announcement.

steps.whatToRemember: This is a proof based on the practical custom of the court of מַר שְׁמוּאֵל.
steps.whyThisMatters: It reinforces שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling with practical precedent.

phrases.english: Rav Naḥman said:

phrases.aramaic: אַבָּא מִן סָפְרֵי דַּיָּינֵי דְּמָר שְׁמוּאֵל הֲוָה,
phrases.english: My father was one of the scribes of the judges of Mar Shmuel,
and I was about six or seven years old,
and I remember that they made an announcement, saying:

phrases.aramaic: הָנֵי שְׁטָרֵי אַקְנְיָיתָא דְּמִשְׁתַּכְחִי בְּשׁוּקָא, נַהְדְּרִינְהוּ לְמָרַיְיהוּ.
phrases.english: Those deeds of transfer that are found in the marketplace should be returned to their owners.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רב נחמן: אבא מן ספרי דייני דמר שמואל הוה...

steps.title: רַב אַמְרָם's proof from the מִשְׁנָה
steps.stepSummary: Rav Amram cites a Mishnah stating that any court enactment must be returned, implying no concern for repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אַמְרָם brings a textual proof from the מִשְׁנָה to support the idea that we don't worry about repayment. This is a proof step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב אַמְרָם cites the מִשְׁנָה: כָּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין – הֲרֵי זֶה יַחֲזִיר. He concludes that we are not concerned about repayment. This seems to support שְׁמוּאֵל's dismissal of the repayment concern.

keyTerms.meaning: Any enactment of the court.
Apparently / it implies.

steps.whatToRemember: The מִשְׁנָה's broad language is used to prove that repayment is not a concern.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up a challenge to שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling, as the מִשְׁנָה seems to apply to all court documents, including debt notes.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב עַמְרָם: אַף אֲנַן תְּנֵינָא.
phrases.english: Rav Amram said: We, too, learn similarly in a mishna
One must return any court enactment
to its owner.
Apparently, we are not concerned that there may have been repayment.
Rabbi Zeira said to him:

The mishna is not proof for Shmuel’s ruling, as it is stated not with regard to all court enactments but with regard to bills of foreclosure, and bills of authorization to locate and seize property from the debtor
both of which are not subject to repayment.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רב עמרם: אף אנן תננא. כל מעשה בית דין – הרי זה יחזיר...

rashi.hebrew: כל מעשה ב"ד - קס"ד שטר שכתב בו הנפק שנתקיים בב"ד אלמא כיון דליכא למיחש לשמא לא לוה שהרי אין מקיימין את השטר אלא בפני בעל דין כדאמרי' בהגוזל (ב"ק דף קיב:) לפרעון לא חיישינן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that Rav Amram's proof assumes the Mishnah refers to a loan document validated in court, where repayment concerns are irrelevant because the court only validates documents in the presence of the defendant.

rashi.hebrew: בשטרי חלטאתא - שטר שכתבו בי"ד למוציא שטר על חבירו וחייבוהו לשלם ולא שילם וירדו לנכסיו ושמו לבעל חוב אחד מהם ונתנו לו שטר שעל פי בי"ד באה לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines שטרי חלטאתא as a court-issued document given to a creditor after the debtor's assets have been seized and assigned to that specific creditor.

rashi.hebrew: אדרכתא - שלא מצאו לו עכשיו נכסים וכתבו לו שירדוף לחזור על נכסיו ואם ימצא יגבה. אדרכתא לשון רודף ומשיג כמו פרסה בחלא ולא אדרכיה (כתובות דף ס:) ולשון המקרא כתרו את בנימין הרדיפוהו מנוחה הדריכוהו (שופטים כ׳:מ״ג):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines אדרכתא as a document issued when no assets are currently found, authorizing the creditor to pursue and collect from the debtor's future assets.

tosafot.hebrew: בשטרי חלטאתא ואדרכתא דלאו בני פרעון נינהו - תימה שטרי אדרכתא אמאי לאו בני פרעון נינהו כיון דעדיין לא זכה בקרקע עד שכתבו לו שטרי חלטאתא וכן בסמוך דקאמר איהו דאפסיד אנפשיה דבעי ליה למקרעיה או אבעי ליה למכתב שטר מכירה בשטרי אדרכתא שעדיין הקרקע היא בידו במה אפסיד אנפשיה הלא יכול לטעון פרעתי וי"ל דשטרי אדרכתא נקט אליבא דרבה דאית ליה בפרק המפקיד (לקמן בבא מציעא דף לה: ושם) דאכיל פירי מכי מטא אדרכתא לידיה א"כ לא יוכל לומר עוד פרעתי כיון שכבר זכה בקרקע ולרבא דאית ליה מכי שלמו יומי דאכרזתא לדבריו יעמיד מתני' בחלטאתא גרידא ויש ספרים דל"ג אדרכתא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why אדרכתא is considered not subject to repayment concerns, since the debtor still owns the land and could claim repayment, and resolves this by citing רבה's view that the creditor acquires rights to the produce upon receiving the אדרכתא, preventing a repayment claim.

steps.title: רַבָּא challenges the claim that foreclosure bills are not subject to repayment
steps.stepSummary: Rava challenges Rabbi Zeira's claim by citing sources that foreclosure bills can indeed be repaid and the property returned.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא attacks רַבִּי זֵירָא's resolution by proving that foreclosure bills ARE subject to repayment. This is a challenge step.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא asks: And are these bills not subject to repayment? He brings two proofs: 1) The חֲכָמִים of נֵהָרְדָּעַי say that property seized can be returned if the debtor pays within twelve months. 2) אַמֵּימָר says that even after twelve months, the property can always be returned if the debt is paid. This directly contradicts רַבִּי זֵירָא's claim that these bills are not subject to repayment.

keyTerms.meaning: The Sages of נֵהָרְדָּעַי.

keyTerms.term: שׁוּמָא הָדַר עַד תְּרִיסַר יַרְחֵי שְׁתָּא
keyTerms.meaning: The appraised property can be returned within twelve months.
The appraised property can be returned forever.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבָּא proves that שִׁטְרֵי חֲלָאָתָא and אֲדַרְכְּתָא ARE subject to repayment, undermining רַבִּי זֵירָא's resolution.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גְּמָרָא to find a new explanation for why the מִשְׁנָה requires returning these documents.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא: וְהָנֵי לָאו בְּנֵי פֵרָעוֹן נִינְהוּ?
phrases.english: And are these bills not subject to repayment?
But didn’t the Sages of Neharde’a say that

phrases.aramaic: שׁוּמָא הָדַר עַד תְּרֵיסַר יַרְחֵי שַׁתָּא.
phrases.english: after property is repossessed in order to pay an unpaid debt based on the court’s appraisal of its value, the property is returned if the debtor pays the debt until twelve months of the year have passed after the repossession?
And furthermore, Ameimar said: I am from Neharde’a,
and nevertheless, I hold that property repossessed based on an appraisal of an article’s value can always be returned.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רבא: והני לאו בני פרעון נינהו? והא אמרי נהרדעי...

rashi.hebrew: שומא - ששמו ב"ד לבעל חוב קרקע הלוה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that שומא refers to the court's act of assigning the debtor's land to the creditor as security for the debt.

rashi.hebrew: הדר - אם יפרע לו מעות עד שנה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that הדר means the debtor can redeem the property by repaying the debt within one year.

steps.title: רַבָּא's resolution: the debtor's negligence
steps.stepSummary: Rava explains that the Mishnah requires returning these documents because the debtor is at fault for not tearing them up or getting a new document upon repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא offers a new resolution for why the מִשְׁנָה requires returning foreclosure bills. He argues the debtor is at fault for not invalidating the old document upon repayment.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא explains that the debtor should have torn up the שְׁטָר or demanded a new one for the redeemed property. By strict law, the land does not require return, but the principle of ועתה תעשה היראה והטוב mandates it, effectively treating it as a new purchase requiring a new document.

keyTerms.meaning: He himself caused the loss / he is at fault.
By strict law, the land does not need to be returned.
And now you shall do that which is right and good.

steps.whatToRemember: The debtor bears responsibility for ensuring the old document is invalidated or replaced upon repayment.
steps.whyThisMatters: This shifts the blame to the debtor for not taking proper steps to invalidate the old document.

phrases.english: Rather, Rava said
There, this is the reason:
As I can say that if the debtor has already repaid his debt, it is he who caused the loss to himself

phrases.aramaic: דִּבְעִידָּנָא דְּפַרְעֵיהּ אִבְּעִי לֵיהּ לְמִקְרְעֵיהּ לִשְׁטָרֵיהּ,
phrases.english: as at the time he repaid his debt he should have either ripped up the document

phrases.aramaic: אִי נָמֵי לְמִכְתַּב שְׁטָרָא אַחֲרִינָא עִילָּוֵיהּ.
phrases.english: or alternatively, he should have demanded of the creditor to write another document for the debtor’s redeemed property, returning it to him.
The reason for a new document to be written is that according to the letter of the law, the land need not be returned by the creditor to the debtor
and it is due to the principle: “You shall do that which is right and good in the eyes of the Lord”
that the Sages said that the land should be returned.
Therefore, it is as though the debtor is purchasing it anew
and the creditor must write a bill of sale.

steps.triggerLanguage: אלא אמר רבא, התם היינו טעמא: דאמרי אייהו הוא דאפסיד אנפשיה...

rashi.hebrew: אפסיד אנפשיה - אם פרע ולא קרע השטר ואם דחהו לומר אבד היה לו לומר כתוב לי שטר מיד שאתה חוזר ומוכרה לי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the debtor's negligence (אפסיד אנפשיה) consists of failing to tear up the original note upon repayment or, if the creditor refused to return it, failing to demand a new document immediately when the creditor re-sold the land.

rashi.hebrew: דמדינא ארעא לא בעי למיהדר - טעם נותן לדבריו שראוי לכתוב שטר מכירה עליה שקנויה לו לחלוטין ומדינא לא בעי למיהדר בפדיון אם לא מדעתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that since strict law (מדינא) does not require the land to be returned upon redemption, the creditor retains full ownership, justifying the need for a new bill of sale.

rashi.hebrew: מרישא דקא זבין - כלומר אין זה פדיון אלא תחילת קניין כאילו שלו היתה לפיכך הכותב שטר מכירה עליה אינו טועה והיה לו לזה לתבוע שטר מכירה הואיל ודחהו מלהחזיר לו שטר של ב"ד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the transaction is treated as a new acquisition (תחילת קניין) rather than a redemption, meaning the debtor should have demanded a new bill of sale since the creditor refused to return the original court document.

steps.title: Challenge: why not apply this to regular debt notes?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks why the logic of the debtor being at fault doesn't apply to regular promissory notes, where the debtor should also have torn up the note upon repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גְּמָרָא raises a clarifying question about the scope of רַבָּא's explanation. It asks why this logic of debtor negligence doesn't apply to regular debt notes.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גְּמָרָא asks: regarding a regular שְׁטַר חוֹב, if the debtor repaid it, he should have torn it up. Why don't we apply the same logic that the debtor is at fault to regular debt notes, as we did for foreclosure bills?

keyTerms.meaning: A regular promissory note / debt note.

steps.whatToRemember: This question sets up the distinction between foreclosure bills and regular debt notes.
steps.whyThisMatters: This leads to the explanation of why regular debt notes are treated differently.

phrases.aramaic: גַּבֵּי שְׁטַר חוֹב מַאי אִיכָּא לְמֵימַר?
phrases.english: With regard to a found promissory note, what is there to say to justify returning it to the creditor?

phrases.aramaic: אִם אִיתָא דְּפַרְעֵיהּ, אִיבְּעִי לֵיהּ לְמִיקְרְעֵיהּ לִשְׁטָרֵיהּ.
phrases.english: That if it is so that the debtor repaid the debt, he should have ripped up the promissory note?

phrases.aramaic: אֵימוֹר אִשְׁתְּמוֹטֵי קָא מִשְׁתְּמִיט לֵיהּ, דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: This is not so, as one could say that the creditor avoided returning the note, as he said to him:

phrases.aramaic: לִמְחַר יָהֵבְנָא לָךְ דְּהַשְׁתָּא לֵיתֵיהּ גַּבַּאי,
phrases.english: Tomorrow I will give you the note, as it is not with me now.

phrases.aramaic: אִי נָמֵי אַפְּשִׁיטֵי דְסָפְרָא זָיֵיר לֵיהּ.
phrases.english: Alternatively, the creditor may have held back the note as security for the debtor’s payment of the fee of the scribe who wrote the promissory note.

steps.triggerLanguage: גבי שטר חוב מאי איכא למימר? אם איתא דפרעיה, איבעי ליה למיקרעיה לשטריה.

rashi.hebrew: אי נמי אפשיטי דספרא זייר ליה - שעל הלוה ליתן שכר הסופר ופעמים שאין לו מעות ללוה בשעת הלואה והמלוה נותן לו שכר לכתיבת השטר וכשפרעו מעכב השטר בידו עד שיפרע השכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the debtor might not have torn up the שטר חוב because the lender withheld it to collect the סופר's fee, which the debtor owes but lacked the funds to pay at the time of the loan.

rashi.hebrew: זייר - לשון עוצר כמו מעצרתא זיירא (ע"ז דף ס.) ובלשון המקרא ויזר את הגזה (שופטים ו׳:ל״ח):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Aramaic term זייר means to withhold or detain, derived from biblical and Talmudic usage.

steps.title: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling on returning a found debt note
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Yochanan rules that a found debt note should not be returned, even if it has a court ratification, due to concern for repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya shifts to רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling, which contrasts with שְׁמוּאֵל's. רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ cites רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן regarding found debt notes.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן rules that one who finds a debt note in the market should not return it to the owner, even if it has court ratification (הַנָּק) written on it. This directly contradicts שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling that we don't worry about repayment.

keyTerms.meaning: Court ratification / approval.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן holds that we DO worry about repayment for debt notes, even with court ratification.
steps.whyThisMatters: This introduces a major opposing view to שְׁמוּאֵל's ruling.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבִּי אֲבָהוּ אָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן:
phrases.english: Rabbi Abbahu says that Rabbi Yoḥanan says:
With regard to one who finds a promissory note in the marketplace,

phrases.aramaic: אַף עַל פִּי שֶׁכָּתוּב בּוֹ הֶנְפֵּק –
phrases.english: even if a ratification of the court is written in it,
he may not return it to the owner.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רבי אבהו אמר רבי יוחנן: המוצא שטר חוב בשוק, אף על פי שכתוב בו הנק – לא יחזירו לבעלים.

tosafot.hebrew: אע"פ שכתוב בו הנפק לא יחזיר - תימה דמשמע דאיירי באין חייב מודה כדאמר שמא כתב ללות ולא לוה לכך לא יחזיר דחיישינן לפרעון אבל אם לוה מודה יחזיר דלא חיישינן לקנוניא ולא לשמא פרעו ורוצה לחזור וללות בו כדי להרויח פשיטי דספרא וקשה דלקמן (בבא מציעא דף יז.) א"ר יוחנן שטר שכתוב זמנו בו ביום וכתוב בו הנפק יחזיר ומוקי לה רב כהנא כשחייב מודה ודוקא שכתוב בו ביום אבל אין כתוב בו ביום לא יחזיר או משום פשיטי דספרא או משום קנוניא וכן לעיל (בבא מציעא דף יג:) אוקי ר"י מתני' דמצא שטרי חוב לא יחזיר בחייב מודה ומשום קנוניא דסברי רבנן דאין בו אחריות גבי (לוה) נמי ממשעבדי (ותניא כוותיה דרבי יוחנן) וי"ל דאמוראי נינהו ואליבא דרבי יוחנן דרב כהנא יעמיד הכא מלתא דרבי יוחנן אף כשחייב מודה ומשום קנוניא ורבי אבהו דמפרש דוקא כשאין חייב מודה אבל כשחייב מודה יחזיר דלא חייש לקנוניא יעמיד נמי מתני' דלעיל כשחייב מודה ומשום שמא כתב ללות ולא לוה כרב אסי אבל היכא דליכא למיחש לכתב ללות ולא לוה כמו בשטרי הקנאה או כתוב בו הנפק כי הכא יחזיר דלא חיישינן לקנוניא וגמרא דנקט לעיל טעמא דקנוניא סבר כאביי דאמר עדיו בחתומיו זכין לו או אקרי וכתיב לא אמרינן:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות resolves the apparent contradiction in רבי יוחנן's rulings by distinguishing between two Amoraic interpretations: רב כהנא holds that רבי יוחנן requires returning the note even when the debtor admits the debt due to concerns of קנוניא, while רבי אבהו holds that רבי יוחנן only forbids return when the debtor does not admit the debt, but permits return when the debtor admits it because there is no concern of קנוניא.

steps.title: Clarifying the scope of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara clarifies that Rabbi Yochanan's ruling applies even when the note has court ratification, because we worry about repayment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גְּמָרָא explains the implications of רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling. It clarifies that the concern about repayment applies even to ratified notes.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גְּמָרָא explains: It is not necessary to say that one should not return a note without court ratification, as in that case one could say the debtor wrote it but didn't borrow (כָּתַב לִלְווֹת וְלֹא לוָה). But even if it has court ratification, one should not return it because we are concerned about repayment (חַיְּשִׁין לִפְרָעוֹן).

keyTerms.meaning: He wrote it to borrow but did not borrow.
We are concerned about repayment.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's concern about repayment applies even to ratified notes.
steps.whyThisMatters: This clarifies that רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling is stronger than it initially appears.

phrases.aramaic: לָא מִיבַּעְיָא הֵיכָא דְּלֹא כָּתוּב בּוֹ הֶנְפֵּק,
phrases.english: It is not necessary to say that one should not return the promissory note in a case where a ratification is not written in it,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִיכָּא לְמֵימַר כָּתַב לִלְוֹת וְלֹא לָוָה.
phrases.english: as in that case there is room to say that the debtor wrote it intending to borrow money, but he did not end up borrowing it.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא אֲפִילּוּ כָּתוּב בּוֹ הֶנְפֵּק, וּמַאי נִיהוּ?
phrases.english: But even if a ratification is written in it, one should not return it. And what is this authorization?

It is an approval that the promissory note has been ratified by the court, which examined the note and the signatures of the witnesses and found everything to be in order. The reason one may not return the promissory note to the creditor is that we are concerned that repayment has already taken place.

steps.triggerLanguage: לא מיבעיא היכא דלא כתוב בו הנק, דאיכא למימר כתב ללוות ולא לוה...

steps.title: רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה challenges רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן from the מִשְׁנָה
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Yirmeya challenges Rabbi Yochanan's ruling by citing the Mishnah that requires returning all court enactments.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה raises an objection to רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's ruling from the מִשְׁנָה. He cites the מִשְׁנָה's broad language regarding court enactments.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה cites the מִשְׁנָה: כָּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין הֲרֵי זֶה יַחֲזִיר. This seems to contradict רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן, as the מִשְׁנָה implies that even court-ratified notes must be returned, suggesting no concern for repayment.

keyTerms.meaning: Any enactment of the court.

steps.whatToRemember: The מִשְׁנָה's broad language is used to challenge רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's concern about repayment.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן's side to limit the scope of the מִשְׁנָה.

phrases.english: Rabbi Yirmeya raised an objection to Rabbi Abbahu from the mishna that states (20a):

phrases.aramaic: כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין הֲרֵי זֶה יַחְזִיר!
phrases.english: One must return any court enactment, i.e., a promissory note that has been authenticated by the court, to its owner.
Rabbi Abbahu said to him: Yirmeya, my son,

phrases.aramaic: לֹא כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין שָׁוִים,
phrases.english: not all court enactments are equal.

Rather, the ruling that one must return such a document applies only in a case where the debtor has the presumptive status of one who denies his debts, and therefore, if he claims the debt was repaid, his claim is not accepted.

steps.triggerLanguage: איתיביה רבי ירמיה לרבי אבהו: כל מעשה בית דין הרי זה יחזיר!

rashi.hebrew: כל מעשה ב"ד - והאי נמי מעשה ב"ד הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי ירמיה's objection is based on the premise that the court's ratification of the loan note constitutes a מעשה בית דין, which according to the Mishnah requires the lender to return the document.

rashi.hebrew: שהוחזק - לוה זה כפרן פעם אחרת לפיכך אין נאמן לומר פרעתי ובההיא תנן יחזיר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that רבי אבהו's response distinguishes between cases, specifying that the Mishnah's rule applies only when the borrower has been established as a כפרן, meaning he is not believed to claim repayment, unlike the current case.

steps.title: רַבָּא challenges the assumption about known deniers
steps.stepSummary: Rava challenges the idea that a debtor who has denied a debt once will never repay again.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא attacks רַבִּי אַבָּהוּ's resolution by questioning the assumption about debtors who deny debts. He challenges the reliability of the 'known denier' distinction.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא asks: Just because a debtor assumed the status of a denier once, does it mean he will never repay a debt again? This challenges the assumption that a known denier's claim of repayment is never believed.

keyTerms.meaning: He has a presumptive status of one who denies debts.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבָּא questions the reliability of the 'known denier' distinction.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the גְּמָרָא to find a new explanation for the מִשְׁנָה.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רָבָא: וּמִשּׁוּם דְּהוּחְזַק כַּפְרָן חֲדָא זִמְנָא תּוּ
phrases.english: But does it necessarily follow that just because a debtor assumed the presumptive status of one who denies his debts after one time that he did so
he will never again repay a debt that he owes, and therefore the promissory note should be returned to his creditor?
Rather, Rava said:

phrases.aramaic: מַתְנִיתִין בִּשְׁטַר חַלְטָאתָא וְאַדְרַכְתָּא, וְכִדְרַבִּי זֵירָא.
phrases.english: The mishna is referring to a bill of foreclosure, or a bill of authorization, which are not subject to repayment, in accordance with the explanation of Rabbi Zeira.

steps.triggerLanguage: אמר רבא: ומשום דהוחזק כפרן חדא זימנא תו לא פרע כלל?!

rashi.hebrew: ותו לא פרע כלל - בתמיה ואי חיישינן לפרעון זה שלא נזהר לשמור שטרו יפסיד:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבה's question implies that if we were to disbelieve a known denier, a lender who was negligent in safeguarding his שטר would suffer a total loss.

tosafot.hebrew: ומשום דהוחזק כפרן תו לא פרע כלל - ואף על גב דאמרינן לקמן (בבא מציעא דף יז.) הוחזק כפרן לאותו ממון הכא דנפל שאני דמוכחא מלתא דמשום הכי לא היה נזהר לשומרו לפי שפרעו הלוה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that רבה's challenge applies specifically to a lost שטר, where the lender's negligence in guarding it indicates he likely already repaid the debt, distinguishing this case from the general rule that a known denier is only disbelieved regarding that specific money.

steps.title: Transition to a new topic about debtors who deny debts
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara transitions to a new topic, citing a teaching about debtors who deny debts.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya transitions to a new topic, citing a teaching about debtors who deny debts. This marks the end of the current sugya and the beginning of a new one.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גְּמָרָא says: And with regard to the topic of one who denies his debts, since it came to us, let us say something about it. It cites רַב יוֹסֵף בַּר מִנְיוּמִי in the name of רַב נַחְמָן regarding a court ruling against a litigant.

keyTerms.meaning: One who denies debts.

steps.whatToRemember: This is a transition to a new sugya about debtors who deny debts.
steps.whyThisMatters: This marks the end of the current sugya and the beginning of a new one.

phrases.aramaic: וְכַפְרָן, הוֹאִיל וַאֲתָא לִידַן נֵימָא בֵיהּ מִלְּתָא.
phrases.english: And with regard to the topic of one who denies his debts, since it came to us, let us say something about it.

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר רַב יוֹסֵף בַּר מִנְיוֹמֵי אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן,
phrases.english: As Rav Yosef bar Minyumi said that Rav Naḥman said:
If the court said to the litigant against whom they ruled: Go and give the other litigant what you owe him,

steps.triggerLanguage: וכפרן, הואיל ואתא לידן נימא ביה מלתא. דאמר רב יוסף בר מניומי אמר רב נחמן...

rashi.hebrew: צא תן לו - שפסקו לו דינו בב"ד שציוו עליו לתת לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the court's command צא תן לו means they have already ruled in favor of the claimant and are ordering the defendant to pay.
